heading: National research and development strategy for distributed ledger technology
label: 19222 National research and development strategy for distributed ledger technology
notes: References in TextExecutive Order 14067, referred to in subsec. (b)(1)(B)(ii)(I), (3), is Ex. Ord. No. 14067, , 87 F.R. 14143, which is set out as a note preceding , Money and Finance.Mar. 9, 2022section 5101 of Title 31

CodificationSection was enacted as part of the James M. Inhofe National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2023, and not as part of the Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act which in part comprises this chapter.

children.heading: Director
children.label: 1 Director
children.text: Except as otherwise expressly provided, the term “Director” means the Director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy.

children.label: A
children.text: is shared across a set of distributed nodes, which are devices or processes, that participate in a network and store a complete or partial replica of the ledger;

children.label: B
children.text: is synchronized between the nodes;

children.label: C
children.text: has data appended to it by following the ledger’s specified consensus mechanism;

children.label: D
children.text: may be accessible to anyone (public) or restricted to a subset of participants (private); and

children.label: E
children.text: may require participants to have authorization to perform certain actions (engaging) or require no authorization (permissionless).

children.heading: Distributed ledger technology
children.label: 3 Distributed ledger technology
children.text: The term “distributed ledger technology” means technology that enables the operation and use of distributed ledgers.

children.heading: Institution of higher education
children.label: 4 Institution of higher education
children.text: The term “institution of higher education” has the meaning given the term in .section 1001 of title 20

children.label: A
children.text: the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation of the Senate; and

children.label: B
children.text: the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology of the House of Representatives.

children.heading: Smart contract
children.label: 6 Smart contract
children.text: The term “smart contract” means a computer program stored in a distributed ledger system that is executed when certain predefined conditions are satisfied and wherein the outcome of any execution of the program may be recorded on the distributed ledger.

children.heading: National distributed ledger technology research and development strategy
children.label: b National distributed ledger technology research and development strategy
A
children.text: Current efforts and coordination by Federal agencies to invest in the research and development of distributed ledger technologies and their applications, including through programs like the Small Business Innovation Research program, the Small Business Technology Transfer program, and the National Science Foundation’s Innovation Corps programs.

children.label: I
children.text: lower transactions costs and facilitate new types of commercial transactions;

children.label: II
children.text: protect privacy and increase individuals’ data sovereignty;

children.label: III
children.text: reduce friction to the interoperability of digital systems;

children.label: IV
children.text: increase the accessibility, auditability, security, efficiency, and transparency of digital services;

children.label: V
children.text: increase market competition in the provision of digital services;

children.label: VI
children.text: enable dynamic contracting and contract execution through smart contracts;

children.label: VII
children.text: enable participants to collaborate in trustless and disintermediated environments;

children.label: VIII
children.text: enable the operations and governance of distributed organizations;

children.label: IX
children.text: create new ownership models for digital items; and

children.label: X
children.text: increase participation of populations historically underrepresented in the technology, business, and financial sectors.

children.label: I
children.text: additional risks that may emerge from distributed ledger technologies, as identified in reports submitted to the President pursuant to Executive Order 14067, that may be addressed by research and development;

children.label: II
children.text: software vulnerabilities in distributed ledger technologies and smart contracts;

children.label: III
children.text: limited consumer literacy on engaging with applications of distributed ledger technologies in a secure way;

children.label: IV
children.text: the use of distributed ledger technologies in illicit finance and their use in combating illicit finance;

children.label: V
children.text: manipulative, deceptive, and fraudulent practices that harm consumers engaging with applications of distributed ledger technologies;

children.label: VI
children.text: the implications of different consensus mechanisms for digital ledgers and governance and accountability mechanisms for applications of distributed ledger technologies, which may include decentralized networks;

children.label: VII
children.text: foreign activities in the development and deployment of distributed ledger technologies and their associated tools and infrastructure; and

children.label: VIII
children.text: environmental, sustainability, and economic impacts of the computational resources required for distributed ledger technologies.

children.label: i
children.text: improve the efficiency and effectiveness of privacy-preserving data sharing among Federal agencies and with State, local, territorial, and Tribal governments;

children.label: ii
children.text: promote government transparency by improving data sharing with the public;

children.label: iii
children.text: introduce or mitigate risks that may threaten individuals’ rights or broad access to Federal services;

children.label: iv
children.text: automate and modernize processes for assessing and ensuring regulatory compliance; and

children.label: v
children.text: facilitate broad access to financial services for underserved and underbanked populations.

children.label: D
children.text: Ways to support public and private sector dialogue on areas of research that could enhance the efficiency, scalability, interoperability, security, and privacy of applications using distributed ledger technologies.

children.label: E
children.text: The need for increased coordination of the public and private sectors on the development of voluntary standards in order to promote research and development, including standards regarding security, smart contracts, cryptographic protocols, virtual routing and forwarding, interoperability, zero-knowledge proofs, and privacy, for distributed ledger technologies and their applications.

children.label: F
children.text: Applications of distributed ledger technologies that could positively benefit society but that receive relatively little private sector investment.

children.label: G
children.text: The United States position in global leadership and competitiveness across research, development, and deployment of distributed ledger technologies.

children.text: Private industry.

children.text: Nonprofit organizations, including foundations dedicated to supporting distributed ledger technologies and their applications.

children.text: State governments.

children.text: Small, medium, and large businesses.

children.label: iii
children.text: Subject matter experts representing multiple industrial sectors.

children.label: iv
children.text: A demographically diverse set of stakeholders.

children.heading: Coordination
children.label: 3 Coordination
children.text: In carrying out this subsection, the Director shall, for purposes of avoiding duplication of activities, consult, cooperate, and coordinate with the programs and policies of other relevant Federal agencies, including the interagency process outlined in section 3 of Executive Order 14067 (87 Fed. Reg. 14143; relating ensuring responsible development of digital assets).

children.label: A
children.text: Priorities for the research and development of distributed ledger technologies and their applications.

children.label: B
children.text: Plans to support public and private sector investment and partnerships in research and technology development for societally beneficial applications of distributed ledger technologies.

children.label: C
children.text: Plans to mitigate the risks of distributed ledger technologies and their applications.

children.label: D
children.text: An identification of additional resources, administrative action, or legislative action recommended to assist with the implementation of such strategy.

children.heading: Research and development funding
children.label: 5 Research and development funding
children.text: The Director shall, as the Director considers necessary, consult with the Director of the Office of Management and Budget and with the heads of such other elements of the Executive Office of the President as the Director considers appropriate, to ensure that the recommendations and priorities with respect to research and development funding, as expressed in the national strategy developed under this subsection, are incorporated in the development of annual budget requests for Federal research agencies.

children.label: A
children.text: the implications on trust, transparency, privacy, accessibility, accountability, and energy consumption of different consensus mechanisms and hardware choices, and approaches for addressing these implications;

children.label: B
children.text: approaches for improving the security, privacy, resiliency, interoperability, performance, and scalability of distributed ledger technologies and their applications, which may include decentralized networks;

children.label: C
children.text: approaches for identifying and addressing vulnerabilities and improving the performance and expressive power of smart contracts;

children.label: D
children.text: the implications of quantum computing on applications of distributed ledger technologies, including long-term protection of sensitive information (such as medical or digital property), and techniques to address them;

children.label: E
children.text: game theory, mechanism design, and economics underpinning and facilitating the operations and governance of decentralized networks enabled by distributed ledger technologies;

children.label: F
children.text: the social behaviors of participants in decentralized networks enabled by distributed ledger technologies;

children.label: G
children.text: human-centric design approaches to make distributed ledger technologies and their applications more usable and accessible;

children.label: i
children.text: digital identity, including trusted identity and identity management;

children.label: ii
children.text: digital property rights;

children.label: iii
children.text: delivery of public services;

children.label: iv
children.text: supply chain transparency;

children.label: v
children.text: medical information management;

children.label: vi
children.text: inclusive financial services;

children.label: vii
children.text: community governance;

children.label: viii
children.text: charitable giving;

children.label: ix
children.text: public goods funding;

children.label: x
children.text: digital credentials;

children.label: xi
children.text: regulatory compliance;

children.label: xii
children.text: infrastructure resilience, including against natural disasters; and

children.label: I
children.text: the social, behavioral, and economic implications associated with the growth of applications of distributed ledger technologies, including decentralization in business, financial, and economic systems.

children.heading: Accelerating innovation
children.label: 2 Accelerating innovation
children.text: The Director of the National Science Foundation shall consider continuing to support startups that are in need of funding, would develop in and contribute to the economy of the United States, leverage distributed ledger technologies, have the potential to positively benefit society, and have the potential for commercial viability, through programs like the Small Business Innovation Research program, the Small Business Technology Transfer program, and, as appropriate, other programs that promote broad and diverse participation.

children.heading: Consideration of national distributed ledger technology research and development strategy
children.label: 3 Consideration of national distributed ledger technology research and development strategy
children.text: In making awards under paragraph (1), the Director of the National Science Foundation shall take into account the national strategy, as described in subsection (b)(4).

children.heading: Fundamental research
children.label: 4 Fundamental research
children.text: The Director of the National Science Foundation shall consider continuing to make awards supporting fundamental research in areas related to distributed ledger technologies and their applications, such as applied cryptography and distributed systems.

children.heading: Distributed ledger technology applied research project
children.label: d Distributed ledger technology applied research project

children.heading: Applied research project
children.label: 1 Applied research project
children.text: Subject to the availability of appropriations, the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology, may carry out an applied research project to study and demonstrate the potential benefits and unique capabilities of distributed ledger technologies.

children.label: i
children.text: improve the privacy and interoperability of digital identity and access management solutions;

children.label: ii
children.text: increase the integrity and transparency of supply chains through the secure and limited sharing of relevant supplier information;

children.label: iii
children.text: facilitate broader participation in distributed ledger technologies of populations historically underrepresented in technology, business, and financial sectors; or

children.label: iv
children.text: be of benefit to the public or private sectors, as determined by the Director in consultation with relevant stakeholders;

children.label: B
children.text: solicit and provide the opportunity for public comment relevant to potential projects;

children.label: C
children.text: consider, in the selection of a project, whether the project addresses a pressing need not already addressed by another organization or Federal agency;

children.label: D
children.text: establish plans to mitigate potential risks, including those outlined in subsection (b)(1)(B)(ii), if applicable, of potential projects;

children.label: E
children.text: produce an example solution leveraging distributed ledger technologies for 1 of the applications identified in subparagraph (A);

children.label: F
children.text: hold a competitive process to select private sector partners, if they are engaged, to support the implementation of the example solution;

children.label: G
children.text: consider hosting the project at the National Cybersecurity Center of Excellence; and

children.label: H
children.text: ensure that cybersecurity best practices consistent with the Cybersecurity Frame work of the National Institute of Standards and Technology are demonstrated in the project.

children.heading: Briefings to Congress
children.label: 3 Briefings to Congress
children.text: Not later than 1 year after , the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology shall offer a briefing to the relevant congressional committees on the progress and current findings from the project under this subsection.December 23, 2022

children.heading: Public report
children.label: 4 Public report
children.text: Not later than 12 months after the completion of the project under this subsection, the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology shall make public a report on the results and findings from the project.
